metrics.narrative: Neal and JoAnna articulated operational pain around manual procurement processes and inefficiency but did not quantify savings, cost reduction, headcount impact, or cycle-time improvement on the 2026-04-08 discovery call. The business problem is confirmed (manual, inefficient procurement across acquired sites with fragmented ERPs) but no ROI case, no baseline metrics, and no target outcomes were established. 83 days into the deal, no quantified value case exists. This is a critical gap for a $125k enterprise procurement deal; without metrics the Economic Buyer (still unidentified) cannot justify the spend. The buyer's silence since discovery suggests either deprioritization or evaluation of alternatives, and the absence of a quantified business case makes it impossible to re-anchor value. Recommended move: re-engage Neal and JoAnna with a structured ROI workshop to quantify savings (cycle time, manual effort, supplier consolidation, contract compliance) and map to their stated pain, then use that case to identify and access the Economic Buyer.

champion.narrative: No champion identified in 83 days. Neal Parkinson and JoAnna Garcia have shown no champion behavior: no internal advocacy, no introductions to IT or the Economic Buyer, no pushing back on competitors (status quo is the implicit competitor), no driving the agenda forward, and no inbound pull since the 2026-04-08 discovery call. They are engaged evaluators on that one call, asking good operational questions, but the 83-day silence and lack of response to the rep's 2026-05-07 email and 2026-06-11 outreach indicate passive or deprioritized interest, not active sponsorship. Champion strength: none. A champion at this stage would have coordinated the IT meeting Neal said was necessary, introduced the Economic Buyer, responded to outreach, or requested the cost/timeline deliverables they explicitly asked for. The absence of a champion in a cold, stalled deal is a fatal gap. Recommended move: re-engage Neal and JoAnna with a value-reframe (quantified ROI case) and ask directly for access to the IT lead and the budget holder, then assess whether Neal or JoAnna can be developed into a coach or champion by giving them a tool (the ROI case) to sell internally. If neither can or will advocate, the deal is likely dead and should be qualified out.

competition.narrative: The primary competitor is status quo / do nothing. No third-party vendor (Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) was mentioned on the 2026-04-08 discovery call or in Salesforce. Neal and JoAnna's questions were exploratory and capability-focused, not comparative, suggesting Actylis is in early education mode rather than active multi-vendor evaluation. The absence of urgency, the 83-day silence, and the lack of follow-through on their own explicit asks (cost, timeline, AI demo, IT alignment) all point to status quo as the real competitor: the pain is tolerable, the budget is not secured, and the deal is not prioritized. The risk is that Actylis quietly deprioritizes Zycus, continues with manual processes or a lighter-weight tactical fix, or evaluates alternatives without telling us (political risk from lost access). Our wedge against status quo: Merlin AI for complex chemical-ingredient sourcing (a differentiated capability Neal and JoAnna were curious about but have never seen), plus multi-ERP integration in a single platform (addressing their fragmentation pain). Activation: demonstrate the Merlin AI mechanics on a real Actylis chemical-ingredient use case in a joint session with Neal, JoAnna, and IT, then quantify the ROI (time saved, error reduction, headcount efficiency) to make the cost of inaction tangible and restart momentum. If we cannot secure that session in the next 14 days, the deal is lost to status quo.

identify_pain.narrative: On the 2026-04-08 discovery call, Neal and JoAnna described clear operational pain: Actylis runs a fragmented, manual procurement process across multiple acquired sites, each on a different ERP (Sage X3, Cisco, SAP). This creates inefficiency, lack of spend visibility, slow cycle times, and difficulty enforcing compliance and supplier consolidation. Specific pain points articulated: (1) manual procurement workflows that do not scale post-acquisition, (2) inability to integrate procurement data across ERPs, requiring manual aggregation and reporting, (3) complex sourcing for chemical ingredients with detailed raw-material specifications, where current manual processes are error-prone and slow, and (4) lack of AI-driven automation, limiting their ability to move faster and reduce headcount in procurement operations. The pain is operationally confirmed and grounded in their acquisition-driven growth and IT fragmentation. The pain owner is Neal (Global Purchasing Director), who is accountable for procurement efficiency and spend management across the enterprise. However, the pain has not been quantified (no cost of delay, no error rate, no FTE burden, no cycle-time baseline), and no compelling event or deadline was stated. The absence of urgency and the 83-day silence suggest the pain is real but not yet acute enough to drive action, or the buyer is evaluating alternatives quietly.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified in 83 days. Neal Parkinson (Global Purchasing Director) and JoAnna Garcia (Procurement Manager) are mid-level procurement roles; neither has shown budget authority or C-level access on the discovery call or in subsequent (nonexistent) engagement. Neal is marked Decision Maker in the Salesforce contact role, but his title and behavior (requesting cost breakdown, deferring to IT, no commercial negotiation) suggest he is an influencer or approver, not the budget holder. The discovery call had no discussion of budget, approval chain, or who controls the commercial decision. No VP, no C-level, no CFO, no exec sponsor engaged. In an $125k enterprise SaaS + AI deal (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk), the Economic Buyer is typically a VP of Procurement, CFO, or COO; none are identified. This is a fatal gap at 240 days to close with zero buyer engagement post-discovery. The absence of an EB means no one is championing the deal internally, no budget is secured, and no approval path exists. Recommended move: re-engage Neal and JoAnna to map the org chart, identify who owns the procurement transformation budget, and secure an introduction to that executive with a quantified business case as the door-opener.

decision_criteria.narrative: On the 2026-04-08 discovery call, Neal and JoAnna articulated five implicit evaluation criteria: (1) cost transparency (initial purchase, ongoing licenses, user packages), (2) implementation timeline and go-live speed, (3) AI capability to handle complex chemical ingredient sourcing with detailed raw-material specifications, (4) integration with multiple ERPs simultaneously (Sage X3, Cisco, SAP) with data segregation across acquired sites, and (5) IT alignment and architecture fit during their ongoing IT revamp. These are inferred from their questions and stated concerns, not from a formal RFP or documented BRD. No formal evaluation scorecard, no RFI response, no structured use-case validation has occurred. The criteria are buyer-driven and operationally grounded, but they remain unvalidated: no demo of AI mechanics, no IT architecture review, no implementation plan provided, no pricing shared. 83 days later, none of the five criteria have been addressed, so the buyer cannot score Zycus against them (or against alternatives). This is a partial read because the criteria are known from the discovery call, but the deal has not progressed to validation. Recommended move: structure a follow-on technical validation session with Neal, JoAnna, and Actylis IT to demonstrate Merlin AI on a chemical-ingredient use case, present multi-ERP integration architecture, and walk through an implementation timeline, then use that session to surface any other unstated criteria and confirm the evaluation process.
